text: The surgeon begins the hepatectomy by dissecting and taking down the ligamentum teres and the falciform ligament, exposing the liver for further surgical access.
During the exploratory laparoscopy, the tumor is visibly bulging out of segment four of the liver, exerting pressure downward towards the porta hepatis.
The surgeon opens the pars flaccida of the lesser omentum, exposing the caudate lobe and passing a forcep through the epiploic foramen to receive a nylon tape.

A nylon tape is maneuvered around the portohepatous and exteriorized through a small chest tube in the left upper quadrant, facilitating external manipulation of the Pringle maneuver for later use in the operation.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the left triangular ligament to mobilize the left lateral section of the liver, carefully avoiding the diaphragmatic veins during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the hepatic cable confluence, revealing the two middle hepatic veins along with the left and right hepatic veins as distinct structures outside the liver.
The surgeon begins dissection around the Arantius ligament, elevating the base of the left lateral section away from the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon dissects down to the porta hepatis, taking down part of the hilar plate just below the tumor. The left hepatic duct is observed to be adherent to the tumor and inseparable.

The surgeon dissects the left portal vein away from the barlact to achieve vascular control. A small segment four vessel is ligated as it courses in front of the left hepatic duct, extending towards the tumor.

The surgeon dissects the left hepatic artery near the hepatocellular carcinoma, which is encapsulated by a distinct capsule. The artery is then double clip ligated and divided, allowing operation close to the tumor along natural tissue planes.
The surgeon continues dissection along the groove for the ligamentum venosum, identifying and ligating a small hepatic artery before dividing it.

The surgeon performs dissection between the inferior vena cava and the tumor, pushing the tumor upwards. The areolar tissue in front of the inferior vena cava is visible, allowing the tumor to be elevated away from the vena cava.

The surgeon mobilizes tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, progressing towards the insertion of the left and middle hepatic veins. The left portal vein is then ligated and divided using a stapling device, efficiently securing the structure.

The surgeon continues dissection lateral to the left hepatic vein, with the impression of the vein visible. The dissection proceeds behind the common trunk of the middle and left hepatic veins, revealing the middle hepatic vein arching over the forceps as the surgeon navigates the groove between the vein's insertion and the vena cava. Attempts to encircle the middle hepatic vein insertion are not feasible at this stage.
The surgeon identifies a small, short hepatic vein in front of the inferior vena cava, leading towards the tumor. This vein is clipped and divided during the dissection.
The surgeon works anterior to the right anterior pedicle and the bifurcation of the barlux, which are visibly adherent to the tumor, preventing extra-glissonian control of the right anterior pedicle.

The surgeon works lateral and posterior to the right anterior pedicle, developing the junction between the right anterior and right posterior pedicles. The right posterior portal vein is visible heading off, with the right posterior artery positioned just in front of it.

The surgeon carefully dissects the biliary confluence, attempting to develop a plane between the tumor and the biliary confluence. Slow tissue manipulation reveals a developing plane at the bifurcation of the bile duct, allowing identification of the right posterior duct despite previous poor imaging quality due to tumor compression.

The surgeon switches from a large right-angle instrument to a smaller one to develop the plane between the tumor and bile duct. As the smaller right-angle instrument is maneuvered, it inadvertently creates a hole in the left bile duct.
The surgeon explores the left bile duct without fully identifying the right posterior duct, performing trial dissection to navigate the complex biliary anatomy.

The surgeon attempts to locate the right posterior duct near the confluence or the right anterior duct but is unable to define it. The left hepatic duct is completely divided, and clips are placed on the stump. The right anterior duct is further mobilized, revealing a clear tissue plane that is followed without breaching the tumor capsule.
The surgeon continues to search for the right posterior duct but is unable to locate it. The sheath surrounding the left duct is divided, increasing its mobility, yet no duct is visible.

The portal vein is visibly separated from the tumor and the posterior plate. Cholangiograms are performed through the cystic duct, initially with a bulldog clamp on the right anterior duct, followed by its removal. Neither image reveals the posterior duct, suggesting it enters the left hepatic duct. This insight guides further dissection and enhances understanding of the local anatomy.

The surgeon identifies the posterior duct anomaly, which is located further away from the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts. This anatomical variation is visible in approximately ten percent of cases. The confluence of the bile duct and the right hepatic duct is sacrificed to achieve a better surgical margin.

The final histology specimen shows no tumor in the extra margin specimen. A bulldog clamp is placed across the right anterior portal vein and artery, revealing a clear demarcation line between liver segments five and six, extending visually up to segments seven and eight despite camera clashing.

The surgeon divides the right posterior duct while maintaining the integrity of the tumor capsule. The surgical field is opened at the corner next to the gallbladder between segments five and six, proceeding without the use of a Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon uses an energy device to superficially open the first half centimeter of the liver parenchyma, addressing minor bleeding. With increased mobility, dissection proceeds behind the hepatic veins, focusing on the left hepatic vein. The left lateral section of the liver is visibly folded over on itself during this process.

The surgeon positions the left lateral section to expose the middle hepatic vein and the left hepatic vein. The left hepatic vein is lifted, providing better lateral and superior exposure. The left hepatic vein is seen swinging up and over, separate from the middle hepatic vein. A stapling device with a vascular load is passed underneath the sucker to secure the vein, although the space is tight.

The camera retreats to allow the placement of the stapler without interference, then returns to confirm that no additional tissue is caught before firing. The left hepatic vein is divided, revealing the left side of the middle hepatic vein more clearly. Remaining parenchymal tissue between the stump of the left hepatic vein and the middle hepatic vein is gently dissected away, with the groove between the two middle hepatic veins being dissected from above, viewed laterally.

A right-angle instrument is passed behind the leftmost middle hepatic vein. The middle hepatic vein is then transected using a stapling device with a vascular load. The Pringle Maneuver is applied for the first time.

During the parenchymal transection, the surgeon maintains the Pringle Maneuver for fifteen minutes, then releases it for five minutes to reduce blood loss and enhance visualization of important structures. Laparoscopic tools are utilized to aid in dissection, and an energy device is employed to seal small vessels as they are identified.

The surgeon works closely with laparoscopic cues, occasionally cleaning the camera due to moisture splatter. A tributary of the radiopathic vein from segment five is dissected, clipped, and divided, with the Pringle Maneuver temporarily released.

The surgeon develops a plane behind the anterior pedicle in preparation for transection. A stapling device is placed, and the bulldog clamp and clip on the right hepatic duct are mobilized out of the way before firing.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection with the patient slightly rotated to the left side. Extra posterior liver tissue is removed to ensure an improved margin near the tumor, while maintaining the integrity of the liver capsule.

A nylon tape is wrapped around the future specimen, serving as a visual guide rather than a hanging maneuver, to maintain the surgical path and ensure accurate identification of the right hepatic vein.

The surgeon divides the liver capsule, allowing increased mobility of the future specimen. The specimen is lifted forward, revealing the right hepatic vein. The pseudocapsule is kept with the specimen as surrounding tissues are carefully teased off the right hepatic vein.

The surgeon uses a Thunder bead to manage small tributaries within the liver parenchyma between the right hepatic vein and the right middle hepatic vein. The right middle hepatic vein, thicker than adjacent veins, is visible as it joins the stump of the left middle hepatic vein. A small bleeder on the right hepatic vein is noted but controlled due to pneumoperitoneum. The stapling device is used to transect the major vessel, followed by further dissection of another vein, which is clipped and divided using an energy device.
The surgeon has reached the area behind the tumor, transitioning into normal, non-tumorous liver parenchyma. A clamp crush technique is employed to manipulate the liver tissue.

The surgeon identifies small tributaries of the right portal vein and the right hepatic vein, noting a bridge of tissue away from the tumor. The tissue is thinned further, allowing the use of a stapling device to transect it, with one more fire of the stapler completing the transection.

The specimen is placed in a bag for removal, but remains in the surgical field. The surgeon levels the table and administers extra fluid to the patient. There is slight oozing from the right hepatic vein, which is controlled with local pressure and flow seal. The surgeon explores the right posterior duct in preparation for hepaticojejunostomy.

The surgeon prepares the Roux-Y limb by identifying the ligament of Treitz and dividing the bowel using a vascular stapler to prevent staple line bleeding. The biliary limb is measured to fifty-five to sixty centimeters, and the defect left by the stapler is closed. The biliary limb is brought forward with a laparoscopic grasper and passed behind the transverse colon through the transverse mesocolon. A small midline incision is made for the hepaticojejunostomy, which is performed using an interrupted technique.
